incorrects.description: Student selected 16: counted symbols instead of skip-counting by scale

params.description: Key pulses to draw attention to scale.

steps.text: You counted the symbols. But each symbol stands for 5 books, not 1. Look at the key. Skip-count by 5s.

incorrects.description: Student selected 25: miscounted or added an extra skip-count

params.description: Symbols pulse one at a time to support counting.

steps.text: Count the symbols first. There are 4. Now skip-count by 5s: 5, 10, 15... What comes next?

incorrects.description: Student selected 15: stopped one skip-count short

steps.text: You skip-counted 5, 10, 15. But there are 4 symbols. Count all 4: 5, 10, 15, and one more.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Symbols highlight one at a time as guide counts: 5, 10, 15, 20.

steps.text: Let me show you. I count the symbols: 1, 2, 3, 4. Each symbol means 5 books. So I skip-count: 5, 10, 15, 20. The answer is 20 books. That's how we use the key to read scaled picture graphs.

incorrects.description: Student selected 5: confused items per group with number of groups

params.description: The 4 bags themselves highlight, not the items inside.

steps.text: You're counting what's inside each group. The question asks how many groups. Count the bags themselves.

incorrects.description: Student selected 8: may have added 4 groups + 5 items, or misunderstood the question

steps.text: Look at the bags. We're asking how many bags you see, not how much is inside them. Count the containers.

incorrects.description: Student selected 20: counted total items instead of groups

params.description: The 4 bags themselves highlight, distinguishing containers from contents.

steps.text: You counted all the items. The question asks how many groups. A group is one bag. How many bags are there?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: The 4 bags highlight one at a time in sequence: 1, 2, 3, 4.

steps.text: Let me show you. I count the bags: 1 bag, 2 bags, 3 bags, 4 bags. There are 4 groups. Each group is a bag. The answer is 4 groups.

incorrects.description: Student selected 2: may have miscounted or confused groups with items in one bag

params.description: All 3 bags highlight to draw attention to each group.

steps.text: Count the bags. Each bag is one group. How many bags do you see?

incorrects.description: Student selected 4: counted items in one bag instead of counting groups

steps.text: You counted the items in one bag. The question asks how many groups. Count the bags, not what's inside.

incorrects.description: Student selected 6: may have miscounted or added bags and items incorrectly

steps.text: Look closely at the bags. Each bag is one group. Count them one at a time.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System counts each bag one by one, highlighting them in sequence: 1, 2, 3.

steps.text: Let me show you. I count the bags: one bag, two bags, three bags. Each bag is a group. There are 3 groups. When we ask how many groups, we count the containers, not the items inside.

beats.text: You found equal groups in graphs. You found them in bags. There's a whole lot more hiding. Let's find them.
